elements.sent-2411-001: In 1977, G\'erard Toulouse has proposed a new concept termed as "frustration" in spin systems.
elements.sent-2411-002: Using this definition, several frustrated models have been created and studied, among them we can mention the Villain's model, the fully frustrated simple cubic lattice, the antiferromagnetic triangular lattice.
elements.sent-2411-003: The former models are systems with mixed ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic bonds, while in the latter containing only an antiferromagnetic interaction, the frustration is caused by the lattice geometry.
elements.sent-2411-004: These frustrated spin systems have novel properties that we will review in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-005: One of the striking aspects is the fact that well-established methods such as the renormalization group fail to deal with the nature of the phase transition in frustrated systems.
elements.sent-2411-006: Investigations of properties of frustrated spin systems have been intensive since the 80's.
elements.sent-2411-007: I myself got involved in several investigations of frustrated spin systems soon after my PhD.
elements.sent-2411-008: I have learned a lot from numerous discussions with G\'erard Toulouse.
elements.sent-2411-009: Until today, I am still working on frustrated systems such as skyrmions.
elements.sent-2411-010: In this review, I trace back a number of my works over the years on frustrated spin systems going from exactly solved 2D Ising frustrated models, to XY and Heisenberg 2D and 3D frustrated lattices.
elements.sent-2411-011: At the end I present my latest results on skyrmions resulting from the frustration caused by the competition between the exchange interaction and the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction under an applied magnetic field.

elements.sent-2411-060: In the pairwise Ising and Heisenberg model, the interaction energy between two spins $\mathbf S_i$ and $\mathbf S_j$ is given by $E=-J \left(\mathbf S_i \cdot \mathbf S_j\right)$ where $J>0$ ($J<0$) is the exchange integral giving rise to parallel (antiparallel) spin pairs.
elements.sent-2411-062: For a crystal where the NN interaction is ferromagnetic, the GS of the system corresponds to the spin configuration where all spins are parallel.
elements.sent-2411-063: This is true for any lattice structure.
elements.sent-2411-064: However, if $J$ is antiferromagnetic, the spin configuration of the GS depends on the lattice structure: i) for lattices containing no elementary triangles, such as square lattice and simple cubic lattices, the GS is the configuration in which each spin is antiparallel to its neighbors, namely every bond is fully satisfied (lowest energy); ii) for lattices containing elementary triangles such as the triangular lattice, the FCC and HCP lattices, it is impossible to construct a GS where all bonds have the lowest energy $J$(see Fig. \ref{fig:IntroFP ): The GS does not correspond to the minimum of the interaction of every spin pair.}
elements.sent-2411-065: In this case, one says that the system is frustrated.
elements.sent-2411-066: Let us recall the frustration defined by Toulouse: one considers an elementary cell of the lattice.
elements.sent-2411-067: The lattice cell is in general a polygon formed by faces hereafter called "plaquettes".
elements.sent-2411-068: For example, the elementary cell of the simple cubic (SC) lattice is a cube with six square plaquettes, the elementary cell of the FCC lattice is a tetrahedron formed by four triangular plaquettes.
elements.sent-2411-069: Let $J_{i,j}$ be the interaction between two NN spins of the plaquette.
elements.sent-2411-070: According to the definition of Toulouse \cite{Toulouse , the plaquette is frustrated if the parameter $P$ defined below is negative \begin{equation}P=\prod_{\left<i,j\right>}\mathrm{sign}(J_{i,j}), \label{frust1}\end{equation} where the product is performed over all $J_{i,j}$ around the plaquette.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Two examples of frustrated plaquettes are shown in Fig. \ref{fig:IntroFP : a triangle with three antiferromagnetic bonds and a square with three ferromagnetic bonds and one antiferromagnetic bond.}
elements.sent-2411-072: One sees that if one tries to put Ising spins on those plaquettes, at least one of the bonds around the plaquette will not be satisfied.
elements.sent-2411-073: For vector spins, we show below that in the lowest energy state, each bond is only partially satisfied.
elements.sent-2411-074: We consider another spin system, which can be called frustrated system because each bond is only partially satisfied: this is the case with different kinds of conflicting interactions and the GS does not correspond to the minimum of each kind of interaction.
elements.sent-2411-075: Let us examine a chain of spins where the NN interaction $J_1$ is ferromagnetic while the next NN (NNN) interaction $J_2$ is antiferromagnetic.
elements.sent-2411-076: As long as $|J_2|\ll J_1$, the GS is ferromagnetic: every NN bond is then satisfied but the NNN ones are not.
elements.sent-2411-077: Of course, when $|J_2|$ exceeds a critical value, the ferromagnetic GS is no longer valid (see the helimagnet example below): both the NN and NNN bonds are not fully satisfied.

elements.sent-2411-156: Skyrmions are topologically stable spin structures observed in various materials and theories.
elements.sent-2411-157: They are objects of intensive studies since 2003 after the work of A.. Bogdanov \cite{Bogdanov .}
elements.sent-2411-158: We just cite a few works hereafter.
elements.sent-2411-159: The rapid development of the field of skyrmions is due to the potential applications using skyrmions in spintronics \cite{Fert2013,Leonov,Ezawa,Zhang,Zhang2017,Zhang2020,Zhang2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-160: Skyrmions are generated by various interaction mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-161: They can result from over-frustrated spin systems under an applied magnetic field \cite{Okubo,Hayami or from the Dzyaloshinskii-Moriya interaction (DMI) as seen in \cite{ElHog2018,Sharafullin2019,Sharafullin2020,ElHog2022 among many other works in the literature.}}
elements.sent-2411-162: Note that a single skyrmion consists of a spin pointing down at the center.
elements.sent-2411-163: The surrounding spins turn around the center with incrreasing $z$ component when going away the center.
elements.sent-2411-164: At the boundary, all spins are up.
elements.sent-2411-165: There are two kinds of such arrangement.
elements.sent-2411-166: The first kind is the Bloch-type skyrmion in which the spins rotate in the tangential planes, namely perpendicular to the radial directions, when moving from the core to the periphery.
elements.sent-2411-167: The second kind is the Neel-type skyrmion in which the spins rotate in the radial planes from the core to the periphery \cite{WangKang .}
elements.sent-2411-168: In our model shown below, only the Bloch-type skyrmions are observed.

elements.section-2411-006: Quantum Theory for Non-Collinear Spin Configurations: Green's Function Method
elements.sent-2411-197: The study of elementary excitations in spin systems is one of the main subjects in magnetism \cite {DiepTM .}
elements.sent-2411-198: In magnetic materials, elementary excitations, called "spin waves" or "magnons", dominate the low-temperature properties.
elements.sent-2411-199: Various means of investigation, theoretically and experimentally, such as spin-wave theories and inelastic neutron scattering, have been developped since the 60's for magnetic materials.
elements.sent-2411-200: Theoretically, the theory of magnons based on the Holstein-Primakoff was the most advanced theory, but the validity of this method is limited to the region of very low temperatures \cite{DiepTM .}
elements.sent-2411-201: The first Green's function method introduced by Zubarev in 1960 \cite{Zubarev allowed us to calculate bulk magnetic properties up to the transition temperature $T_C$.}
elements.sent-2411-202: I have applied this method with success in a study of magnetic thin films in 1979 \cite{Diep1979 .}
elements.sent-2411-203: Note that these studies have been limited to the cases where the magnetic ordering is collinear.
elements.sent-2411-205: Quartu a Green's function technique in 1997 to study non-colinear spin configurations \cite{Quartu in frustrated spin systems: we observe that the commmutation relations between spin-deviation operators are valid only when the spin lies on its quantization axis.}
elements.sent-2411-206: However, in a non-collinear configuration, each spin has its own quantization axis.
elements.sent-2411-207: Our idea was, for a given spin, to use its "local coordinates".

elements.sent-2411-301: As seen in this review, frustration is at the origin of many striking phenomena, going from the coexistence of order and disorder in systems at equilibrium to the existence of reentrant phases and disorder lines, passing by systems with non-collinear spin structures and skyrmions.
elements.sent-2411-302: The frustration in materials opens many new areas of physics not only on the theoretical point of view but also on many applications in spintronics, as mentioned above.
elements.sent-2411-303: This paper is just a personal account of the emergence of a modern physics in which various frustrated systems are still actively studied.
elements.sent-2411-304: The author is grateful to his many collaborators and former doctorate students for contributions to works mentioned in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-305: Before introducing the definition of the frustration given by Toulouse \cite{Toulouse in the context of spin glasses, let us recall some early developments in the 60's and early 70's on spin glasses.}
elements.sent-2411-306: The new quenching experimental method introduced by Pol Duwez \cite{Duwez in 1960 has resulted in the fabrication on many metglasses (metallic glasses) in which bond disorder causes metastable states.}
elements.sent-2411-307: The reader is referred to the reference \cite{review for reviews on works up to 1984 on metglasses.}
elements.sent-2411-308: Several theoretical models of spin glasses have been published in the 70's.
elements.sent-2411-309: One can mention two of them: the Edwards-Anderson (EA) model \cite{EAmodel and the Sherrington-Kirpatrick (SK) model \cite{SKmodel .}}
elements.sent-2411-310: Spin glasses are viewed as systems having two main ingredients: bond disorder and frustration.
elements.sent-2411-311: In a spin glass, there is a random mixing of ferromagnetic and antiferromagnetic bonds between spins.
elements.sent-2411-312: This mixing causes the frustration as we willl define below.
elements.sent-2411-313: The EA model consists of nearest-neighbor Ising spins interacting with each other via a pairwise random interaction.
elements.sent-2411-314: This model has been exactly solved by its authors using the replica method with the hypothesis that the random interactions obey a Gaussian distribution covering negative and positive values.
elements.sent-2411-315: The SK model was also based on the Ising spin model, however the spins interact with each other via an infinite range with a Gaussian distribution with positive and negative interactions.
elements.sent-2411-316: The SK model has also been solved by its authors using the replica method, but the entropy is found negative at low temperatures.
